title: Online Class Series + Optional Field Trip: Warblers of Washington, Jewels of the Air with Connie Sidles
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/warblerjewels2026

Online Classes: Wednesdays, June 3 and 10 from 7:00-8:30pm

Optional Field Trip: Sunday, June 14

Early summer is upon us, and each day sees more nesting behavior by the neotropical migrants who have come here from the South to breed. Perhaps the most beautiful of the migrants - both in feathers and song - are the wood warblers. Join master birder Connie Sidles as she shows you how to find and identify these quick-moving bursts of color as they forage for insects and raise their young. Washington has hosted a full three dozen warblers over the years. In two classes. Connie will cover 12 of the most common. Come and be delighted. The optional field trip along the I-90 corridor to Cle Elum and the Ellensburg area is limited to 18 people on a first-come, first signup basis.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Green Lake
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Roniq Bartanen (she/her)

Bike Parking: A small bike rack is available near the coffee shop

Trip Accessibility: 1( leisurely pace), A ( paved and level), ~ 2.8 miles walking total loop

Did you know Green Lake is a rich and diverse urban birding habitat? Over 175 species of birds have been seen at this eBird hot spot! During our outing we will explore migratory bird species as well as the many birds seen here all year long. All levels of birding experience and ages as well as families are welcome.

Green Lake monthly bird outings are inclusive of all ages, gender pronouns, sexual orientations, races, cultures, abilities, ethnicity and socioeconomic status. All participants are free to attend the outing for as long as they like. Our pace will be slow and easy as we navigate 2.8 miles of level terrain.

Dress appropriately for the weather and please leave dogs at home so as to not scare our feathered friends.

A small parking lot is available and gets crowded, please leave time to park. Nearby street parking is also available. Green Lake is bus accessible with buses #62 and #45. A small bike rack is near the Café.

The trail is ADA accessible; all abilities and mobilities are welcome! Restrooms and drinking fountains are available at the nearby Green Lake Community Center Building and at various locations along the trail.

Photo Credit: Roniq Bartanen

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Carkeek Park
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Nathaniel Peters

Meeting Point: Upper/main parking lot recommended for parking. We assemble at the bulletin board by the keyboard crosswalk and railroad footbridge.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), C (hiking trails with gentle rolling hills), 1 mile walking

Masks are optional

In Spring, our forests are green, buzzing with insects, and filled with the songs of passerines. Breeding/nesting activities are well underway, if not already completed. By June, we are near the end of spring and are often treated to baby birds, so we will definitely keep our eyes and ears open for them!

We will begin our June outing with a survey of the Puget Sound from the parking lot followed by a walk of Carkeek's beach to the mouth of Piper's Creek. We will then explore the park's rapidly-shifting wetland area, courtesy of Carkeek's resident master-contractors (i.e., beavers). Whatever the time of year, there are always interesting things to be seen and heard from the birds of Carkeek Park!


The walk will be slow-paced and leisurely, and may include some walking on dirt trails at a low to moderate incline. In Seattle, it is always possible that weather will be cool, windy, and/or rainy, so participants are encouraged to check the weather forecast prior to the walk and to dress/prepare accordingly (e.g., warm clothes, sturdy shoes, and rain gear for wet weather). The leader will have a pair of binoculars, a spotting scope, and a field guide. Participants are encouraged to bring their own binoculars, telescopes, and/or cameras if they have them.

title: Conservation Reading Group: A Natural History of Empty Lots by Christopher Brown
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/conservation-reading-group-a-natural-history-of-empty-lots

The Birds Connect Seattle Conservation Committee invites you to read and learn with us. Together, we'll deepen our understanding of conservation and become stronger advocates for birds and nature.

This quarter, we're reading A Natural History of Empty Lots: Field Notes from Urban Edgelands, Back Alleys, and Other Wild Places by Christopher Brown.

Purchase the book from our shop on bookshop.org https://bookshop.org/shop/birdsconnectsea.org , and the proceeds of the sale will support conservation and other programs at Birds Connect Seattle.

Make sure to follow along on Adabook https://www.addabook.net/book/9781643265223?bsid=120461&refu=birdsconnect&reflid=birdsconnect-L%3Ace309ad60c for prompts and reflections.

Book Description:

A Natural History of Empty Lots is a genre-defying work of nature writing, literary nonfiction, and memoir that explores what happens when nature and the city intersect. To do this, we must challenge our assumptions of nature itself.

During the real estate crash of the late 2000s, Christopher Brown purchased an empty lot in an industrial section of Austin, Texas. The property—a brownfield site bisected with an abandoned petroleum pipeline and littered with concrete debris and landfill trash—was an unlikely site for a home. Along with his son, Brown had explored similar empty lots around Austin, “ruined” spaces once used for agriculture and industry awaiting their redevelopment as Austin became a 21st-century boom town. He discovered them to be teeming with natural activity and embarked on a twenty-year project to live in and document such spaces. There, in our most damaged landscapes, he witnessed the remarkable resilience of wild nature, learned how easy it is to bring back the wild in our own backyards, and discovered that, by working to heal the wounds we have made on the Earth, we can also heal ourselves.

Beautifully written and philosophically hard-hitting, A Natural History of Empty Lots offers a new lens on human disruption and nature, offering a sense of hope among the edgelands.

Learn how the Conservation Reading Group works on our website https://birdsconnectsea.org/learn/conservation-reading-group/ .

title: NextGen Wingspan Night at Bizarre Brewing
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/nextgen-wingspan-game-night-june2026-bizarre

Date/Time: Wednesday. June 17th 6:30-9pm
Location: Bizarre Brewing 4441 26TH AVE W SEATTLE, WA 98199

Join Birds Connect Seattle's NextGen Council for an evening of board game fun, at one of our favorite breweries - Bizarre Brewing!

This event is hosted by NextGen, but gamers of all ages are welcome to register and attend. We will set up the board games and split into groups from ~6:30-6:45pm, and start playing right around 6:45/7pm so we wrap up by 9pm (when the brewery closes).


Wingspan is a massively popular, competitive, medium-weight, card-driven, engine-building board game from Stonemaier Games. It's designed by Elizabeth Hargrave and features over 170 birds illustrated by Beth Sobel, Natalia Rojas, Ana Maria Martinez, and Martha Clare.

In the game (as in real life), you are bird enthusiasts—researchers, bird watchers, ornithologists, and collectors—seeking to discover and attract the best birds to your network of wildlife habitats. Each bird extends a chain of powerful combinations in one of your habitats (actions). Beginners are welcome!

When you arrive, please look for the table tents with our event name on them (Birds Connect Seattle Board Game Night) or ask one of the friendly beertenders for assistance. If you own the Wingspan board game, please bring it to the event, we will need some extra sets of the game and any expansion packs!

Bizarre Brewing: The taproom is all ages and well-behaved, leashed dog friendly. The taproom has two all-gender ADA restrooms. Outside food is welcome. Bizarre also offers snacks for purchase including: Umami Kushi giant hot pretzel twist w/mustard, Damoori Kitchen pita chips + hummus, Bavarian Meats landjaeger, Beecher’s Cheese cheese sticks, Cougar Mountain cookies, and Tim's chips. Alcoholic and non-alcoholic drink options available. Ample street parking nearby.

Please Note: This event has limited slots, and you must RSVP. If the event is full, please signup for the waitlist, and you will be moved up as people update their RSVPs.
location: Bizarre Brewing,4441 26TH AVE W SEATTLE, WA 98199

title: In-Person Classes: Birding by Ear, Tackling the Tricky Ones
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/bbetricky062026

Instructor: Whitney Neufeld-Kaiser

In-Person Classes: Thursdays, June 18 and 25 from 7:00-8:30pm

Class Location: Birds Connect Seattle classroom (enter at 1809 7th Ave, Seattle, WA 98101)

Optional Field Trip: Sunday, June 28, 6:30 AM – 9:30 AM; Place is TBD but will be one of the big parks in the Seattle area

Field Trip Participant Limit: 10

Some pairs and trios of Pacific Northwest birds are difficult to distinguish by sound – at first! Such as American Robin and Black-headed Grosbeak. Using various tools of "birding by ear (BBE)" – careful, analytical listening; sonograms; mnemonics – we’ll tackle some of these tricky ones, highlighting the distinctions and clues that can help tell them apart. Folks who have had some experience with birding by ear will enjoy this class the most; this class will likely be frustrating for people just starting to listen to birds. Reading Nathan Pieplow’s webpage http://earbirding.com/blog/specs http://earbirding.com/blog/specs earbirding.com/blog/specs beforehand is recommended.

title: In-Person Author-Guided Waterfront Nature Walk with Kathryn True and Maria Dolan
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/seattlefieldguidewalk2026

The new Seattle waterfront provides plentiful delights for human visitors, including new bike and walking paths, inspiring artworks and wide-open views. But did you know that this stretch also includes new habitats and hiding places for Seattle's birds, animals and plants?

This walk is drawn from Seattle Field Guide, a new seasonal guidebook to urban nature outings by authors Kathryn True and Maria Dolan. On this one-mile, one-way walk along Seattle's transformed waterfront we'll look for birds, talk about how changes here enhance Chinook salmon migration (and how that affects our local orca populations), check out a kelp forest, and note the PNW artworks that tell natural and cultural stories along the route. End at Overlook Walk, at the entrance to Pike Place Market.

Meet at 199 Alaskan Way, in front of the Historic Washington Street Boat Landing Pergola, in front of Molly Moon's ice cream shop. This is a one way walk and ends at the Overlook Walk near the Seattle Aquarium. The walk will take about 60 minutes. Bring binoculars if you have them, but they aren't required.

Accessibility notes: Flat, paved sidewalk along waterfront. Habitat beach has rocks and sand but is viewable from the sidewalk. Step-free bathroom near the end of the walk.

About us:

Maria Dolan has written on birds, bats, orcas, environmental issues and other subjects for publications including Smithsonian.com, http://Smithsonian.com, CityLab, the Seattle Times, Sierra, Seattle Magazine and more. She lives in Seattle. Visit her online at mariadolan.org http://mariadolan.org or on Instagram bookish_md

Kathryn True embraces her naturalist tendencies through hiking, birding, community science, and swimming in cold mountain lakes. She blogs about the natural world for Vashon Nature Center and authored a Seattle Times series called "Natural Wanders." Her work has also appeared in Sunset and Alaska Airlines Magazine. She lives on Vashon Island, WA. Visit her online at kathryntrue.com http://kathryntrue.com or on Instagram @trueislander63

title: Online Lunch and Learn: All About Tree Canopy Cover Assessments with Joshua Morris
description:
REGISTER HERE https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSeCIdIcFwthRCdx1k29kkSgHMDDSBOSzd-kRJOW-E53dzZXNg/viewform

Tree canopy cover is one of the most widely used indicators for measuring progress toward urban forestry goals and evaluating tree canopy equity across neighborhoods. In this 15-minute presentation, we will review the methods used to assess tree canopy cover along with key challenges practitioners face: distinguishing tree cover from other vegetation, handling seasonal variation, and ensuring consistency across repeated assessments over time. Finally, we will look at how cities in King County use tree canopy cover data to inform urban forest management.

About the speaker: Joshua Morris is Conservation Director at Birds Connect Seattle. Joshua served on Seattle’s Urban Forestry Commission from 2019 to 2025 where he co-developed commission recommendations on updating tree protection ordinances, strengthening urban forestry policies in the City's Comprehensive Plan, improving tracking of urban forest expenditures in the City budget, and more.

This presentation is part of the Understory Lunch and Learn Series, an initiative of the Tree Equity Network https://www.seattleparksfoundation.org/community-partners/tree-equity-network/ offering accessible, concise information and resources on urban forest management, policy, and advocacy. Birds Connect Seattle and Seattle Parks Foundation co-lead the networks.

No mask requirements


Join us as we go birding on the Eastside at Marymoor Park! And yes, you read that right: we are offering dog friendly neighborhood outings!

Well behaved dogs welcome on leash while on trails and off leash as applicable during the off leash dog park section. Please leave dogs at home if they're not friendly or just too excitable towards people, dogs or birds.

Come bird with us in a relaxed setting before the July 4th holiday gets started and fireworks start popping off, scaring off dogs and birds and people alike!

We will walk a large loop around the park, covering a diverse range of habitats including meadows, woods, marsh, the north end of Lake Sammamish, the Sammamish River, and community garden. Birders of all levels and ages are welcome. Note that about half this walk is in the off-leash dog area. We’ll spend about ~3 hours in the field. Bring layers, rain gear, water and snacks. Done around 10:15am or so.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Birds and Habitat – Along Arboretum Creek
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Please leave pets at home.

We walk north approximately 1.5 miles through the Arboretum, often next to or parallel with Arboretum Creek and usually end up near Duck Bay (south of Foster Island). We will be searching for birds and discussing their habitat choices for food, nesting and roosting. We begin at 8:30 am and the trip will end around 10:30 am near Duck Bay – which is about one mile north of the Birch Parking Lot. However, walking south, back to your vehicle, may take another 20-30 minutes depending on each individual’s motivation. To learn more about our efforts to enhance the Arboretum Creek habitat for birds and nature please visit: www.arboretumcreek.org https://www.arboretumcreek.org

Photo Credit: Rod Fung
location: Birch Parking lot, Washington Park Arboretum

title: Summer Membership Drive: Andrew's Story
description:
Donate in support of the next generation of bird enthusiasts.

Birds Connect Seattle’s Summer Membership Drive celebrates the joy, curiosity, and connection that birds inspire across generations.

Board member Andrew Larsen recently shared a story https://birdsconnectsea.org/2025/04/01/birding-across-generations-andrews-story/ about his four-year-old nephew proudly naming birds like Dark-eyed Juncos, Black-capped Chickadees, and hummingbirds—a reminder that birding is about so much more than identifying species. It creates moments of wonder, connection, and stewardship that can last a lifetime, and bring generations closer together.

Your support helps make these experiences possible for young people across our region through Nature Camp, Young Birders, school programs, field trips, and hands-on environmental education.

Join us in inspiring the next generation of bird lovers and conservationists.

Donate Today https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/W2n_j1-J3UW_Oci--0BK2A2

title: Field Trip: Big Four Ices Caves Trail
description:
This trip is at capacity. Click here to register for the WAITLIST https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/bigfourjuly2026 .

Leaders: Neil Zimmerman (he/him) (206 200 6700 day of only) and Roniq Bartanen (she/her)

6:00 AM Greenlake P&R, north side of 65th under I-5

Limit: 10

Trip accessibility: 1B (leisurely pace on soft ground, sand, gravel, dirt roads)

With its diverse habitat, the Big Four area is one of the best bird-watching locales in the North Cascades. The trail is unique because it allows birdwatchers to pass almost effortlessly from lowland marsh, meadow, and river habitats through classic forests, into the Alpine Zone. The birding can be exceptional, expect to see a variety of birds in the picnic and marsh area, including Red-breasted Sapsuckers, Vaux's Swifts, Willow Flycatchers, Common Yellowthroats, Yellow Warblers, and Warbling Vireos. American Dippers, Common Mergansers, Harlequin Ducks, and Spotted Sandpipers can be seen on the river. In recent years, this location has attracted breeding American Redstarts. This is one of the most westerly nesting locations in the country. Return to P&R at 4:00pm.

title: Field Trip: Westcrest Park in Highland Park, Seattle
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/july12westcrest

Leaders: Whitney Neufeld-Kaiser (she/her) and Atul Tandon (he/him)

6:30 AM, Playground Parking Lot, on 8th Ave SW between SW Trenton St. and SW Cloverdale St. Bus #131 stops at 9th Ave SW and SW Trenton St, then it’s less than 10 minutes to walk to the parking lot.

Limit: 12

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), C (sidewalks plus hiking trails with gentle rolling hills plus a few sets of stairs), approx. 1.5 miles walking

Westcrest Park is the southern end of the West Duwamish Greenbelt and includes trails winding through mixed forest, a community garden, and open greenspace over the covered water reservoir. Anyone interested in birds is welcome – the trip leaders will have extra pairs of binoculars for folks who need them. Our route will first meander south through the open space and return via the trees to include different sorts of habitat. We’ll likely encounter Western Flycatchers, Bewick’s Wrens, Spotted Towhees, Red-breasted Nuthatches, Song Sparrows, American Robins. Perhaps Hutton’s Vireo. Pileated Woodpeckers, Cooper’s Hawks, and Common Ravens are always possible. A significant portion of the walk lends itself to finding birds by their sounds, because the tree canopy is high. Our route usually takes about 3 hours, depending on what we see and hear and how many questions spark conversation. End at 9:30 - 10:00 AM.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: NextGen: Sammamish River Trail
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Kanupria Sanu (she/her)

Meeting Point: We will meet directly outside the front doors of the Redmond Library (15990 NE 85th St, Redmond), the entrance facing the parking lot. There is a small plaza area there where everyone can gather comfortably before we head across the street to connect to the trail.

  * Trip Accessibility: 1A. Fully paved, flat, and level trail. Total distance will be roughly 1.5 to 2 miles walking at a very gentle, slow pace focused on mindful observation.
  * Parking: Plenty of free parking is available in the Redmond Library parking lot, including ADA-accessible spots.
  * Bathrooms: Restrooms are available inside the library during operating hours.


Join us for a Mindful Birding Walk along the Sammamish River Trail!

Slow down, engage your senses, and connect with the local environment on this gentle morning walk. Starting from the Redmond Library, we will quickly connect to the Sammamish River Trail, which offers a beautiful, open riparian habitat.


This walk is designed to be a mindful, slow-paced experience where we practice deep listening and observational awareness. The trail is completely paved and flat, making it highly accessible and a wonderful option for birders of all experience levels. Because this habitat blends river ecology with open brush and parkland, we will keep our eyes and ears open for resident and summer birds. We can expect to see or hear a variety of waterfowl and herons by the water, as well as songbirds like song sparrows, American goldfinches, cedar waxwings, black-capped chickadees, and potentially nesting swallows or raptors overhead.


Please bring binoculars if you have them, water, and comfortable walking shoes. No dogs, please, to help us keep a quiet and attentive presence for the birds
location: 15990 NE 85th St, Redmond, WA 98052, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Green Lake
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Roniq Bartanen (she/her)

Meeting Point: Green lake Boathouse in front of the café. 7351 East Green Lake Dr. N, Seattle, WA 98115. Green Lake café hours: Dependent on season

Bike Parking: A small bike rack is available near the coffee shop

Trip Accessibility: 1( leisurely pace), A ( paved and level), ~ 2.8 miles walking total loop

Did you know Green Lake is a rich and diverse urban birding habitat? Over 175 species of birds have been seen at this eBird hot spot! During our outing we will explore migratory bird species as well as the many birds seen here all year long. All levels of birding experience and ages as well as families are welcome.

Green Lake monthly bird outings are inclusive of all ages, gender pronouns, sexual orientations, races, cultures, abilities, ethnicity and socioeconomic status. All participants are free to attend the outing for as long as they like. Our pace will be slow and easy as we navigate 2.8 miles of level terrain.

Dress appropriately for the weather and please leave dogs at home so as to not scare our feathered friends.

*Bring plenty of water and snacks, as this is a longer walk!*

A small parking lot is available and gets crowded, please leave time to park. Nearby street parking is also available. Green Lake is bus accessible with buses #62 and #45. A small bike rack is near the Café.

The trail is ADA accessible; all abilities and mobilities are welcome! Restrooms and drinking fountains are available at the nearby Green Lake Community Center Building and at various locations along the trail.

title: Author Talk with Pam McGaffin - Shade of Wings
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/pam_mcgaffin_shadeofwings

Date/Time: Thursday, July 16, 2026, 6:30pm

Location: Birds Connect Seattle HQ (616 Olive Way)

Join us for an author talk with Pam McGaffin, author of Shade of Wings, a speculative young adult novel about a family of New York City crows struggling to survive the outbreak of West Nile virus during the sizzling summer of 1999.

Four-year-old Duncan needs to hurry up and find a mate—or so says Cloud. But she doesn't know about the mistake that's preventing him from leaving their family to start another.

Though he's the eldest, Duncan doesn't see himself as a leader. Yet that's what he must become when both his parents die of the mysterious illness that's killing crows across New York City. He devotes himself to caring for his siblings, including three fledglings—but he soon discovers he can't protect them from the “blind death.”

Meanwhile, a zoo pathologist's worst fears are realized. It starts with dead flamingos. Then critically ill New Yorkers start showing up in hospital emergency rooms.

Some blame the crows.

Pam will be joined by Dr. Kaeli Swift, crow researcher and specialist from the University of Washington for a post-presentation discussion.

Pam McGaffin is a writer whose work has been published in literary journals, newspapers, and on websites. Her first novel, The Leaving Year, (SparkPress, 2018), won three finalist teen fiction awards and took first place in the 2018 Best Book Awards for young adult fiction. A proud bird nerd, she lives in Seattle, WA, with her husband, Mark, and rescue dog, Mars. On their daily walks, they are greeted by many crow friends.
location: Birds Connect Seattle headquarters and retail space (ground floor presentation area),616 Olive Wy, Seattle, WA 98101, USA

title: Field Trip: Early Morning Owl Prowl at Marymoor Park (for young adults <40)
description:
This trip is now at capacity. Register for the WAITLIST https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/earlyowlprowlmarymoor .

Leaders: Casey Herrick (she/her) and Jen Yu (she/her)

4:45am Marymoor Park Parking Lot G. 6046 West Lake Sammamish Parkway Northeast, Redmond, Washington 98052. $1 to park.

Limit: 10

Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (soft ground, dirt). We will walk at a leisurely pace on mostly flat terrain and cover a distance of around ~2 miles. There are bathrooms at the start and midway through our walk.

Join the NextGen Council as we lead a group through Marymoor in the early morning on a prowl for our local Seattle owls. We encourage you to bring a headlamp (with red light function if possible) for walking in the dark, and warm layers, with good hiking shoes. We will be birding at sunrise and it will be completely dark at the beginning of our walk, please bring headlamps or flashlights to ensure your safety while walking at night.

Our path will take us through an Audubon Bird Loop trail, which is mostly gravel roads with some paved and boardwalks as well. Our path will take us into the off leash dog park at Marymoor, so prepare to be in the presence of dogs. This walk is geared specifically for Young Adults under 40. Birders of all experience levels are welcome. Photography welcome, but no flash photography. End time is 7:00am.

description:

We will take a snack, story, and/or look-at-interesting-rocks break somewhere in the middle depending on the pacing and interest level of the kids. Please no adults not accompanied by a child.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Ballard Locks
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: John Leszczynski & Elaine Chuang

Meeting Point: In front of the Ballard Locks Visitor Center. For those driving, note that on the Ballard side of the Locks, there is a paid parking lot at the main entrance, and limited nearby street parking. There is free parking in a small lot in Commodore Park on the Magnolia side (south side).

Bike Parking: Bike racks near visitor center

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly solid pavement but some steps and steep hills) ~ 1 mil

The meeting of salt and fresh water at the Ballard Locks makes for an interesting mix of species, many of which can be observed at close range. Join us the last Saturday of each month to watch an ever-changing cast of birds. Seasonal visitors integrate with year-round residents like Belted Kingfishers, Cormorants, and Great Blue Herons. Cared for by the horticulture staff, the surrounding grounds are also habitat for “the usual suspects” for our region. This is a great walk for all, including beginners: no experience is necessary! If you can, bring binoculars; they are available on loan from the Birds Connect Seattle Retail Space located at 616 Olive Way, Seattle, WA. Please call 206-523-4483 to reserve a pair.
location: Ballard Locks Visitor Center,3015 NW 54th St, Seattle, WA 98107, USA

Hello fellow Queer Birders! Juanita bay is one of my (Casey’s) favorite birding spots, and is considered one of the birding hotspots of the East side of Lake Washington. This park features views of Forbes Creek Wetland, Juanita Beach and Juanita Bay. It is a Green Kirkland Partnership restoration area. The maximum slope of the trail is moderate, with mostly flat walkways, such as the boardwalks.

We will be staying in relatively close proximity to the car park, with plenty of turn around points along the way. Bathrooms are easily accessible the whole trip.

This walk is geared specifically for anyone who identifies as LGBTQIA+ (Allies are of course welcome too!!) and young adults (under 40). Any level of birding experience is welcome.

description:

Leaders: Anthony Pangelinan (he/him) and Suzannah Yu (she/her) (NextGen Advisory Council Members)
Meeting Point: 9:00 at Juanita Bay Park (2201 Market Street, Kirkland, WA 98033). Meet in parking lot off of Market St. Please note, this is not the same parking lot as Juanita Beach Park.

Bike Parking: There are outdoor bike racks ~0.25 miles from the parking lot, near the boardwalk.
Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), A (mostly level and paved), ~ 2 miles walking. Restrooms at the start of our route. ADA parking available.


Considered one of the birding hotspots of the East side of Lake Washington, this park features views of Forbes Creek Wetland, Juanita Beach and Juanita Bay. It is a Green Kirkland Partnership restoration area. The maximum slope of the trail is moderate, with mostly flat walkways, such as the boardwalks. We'll see a great variety of birds, such as wood ducks, eagles, cormorants, and mergansers, and keep an eye (and ear) out for rare birds like Wilson's Snipe and Virginia Rail in the sandy areas.

We will explore the open meadow in the park before walking to the boardwalks that overlook Juanita Bay. Then we will head towards the Old Market Street Trail boardwalk where we'll slowly work our way down alternatively looking for birds in the lake and beaches on one side and wetlands on the other. We will wrap up the birding portion at 11am.

We will be hosting an optional berry/fruit-themed potluck afterwards. We welcome others to bring any fruity snacks to share with the group, but bringing something is not required to join in. If you do bring anything, please inform others of any allergens and of any nuts/meat/eggs/gluten content. The potluck portion goes from 11am-12pm.

This Neighborhood Bird Outing is specifically designed for BIPOC folks and welcomes birders of all ages experience levels.
location: Juanita Bay Park,2201 Market St, Kirkland, WA 98033, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Shorebirds Galore at həʔapus Village Park
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Kurt Aylup (he/him)

Meet: 9 am, at the parking lot at the west end of the park, near the intersection of SW Edmunds St and W Marginal Way SW

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), Terrain B

Onsite Bike Parking: Unsure about dedicated bike racks, but seems like there are picnic tables/other things like that where people might be able to lock a bike.

If you ask any well-adjusted, sane person what’s the first thing they think of when you mention late summer in Seattle, there’s only one reasonable answer you can expect: SHOREBIRDS. That’s right, August - September is often peak “fall” migration season for shorebirds at our latitude as they venture down from what are often arctic breeding grounds and begin epic migrations across the globe. Most big cities won’t get nearly as many species or individual birds as you might see on more expansive suitable habitat, and Seattle is no exception, but if you look in the right spots at the right times you can still get your annual fix without going too far out of the Seattle city limits, or even out of the city at all!

For those of us who often do their birding closer to home, həʔapus Village Park (aka/fka "T107") is typically the most reliable spot in the city for seeing shorebirds at this time of year. Some species we can expect are Least Sandpiper, Western Sandpiper, Spotted Sandpiper, and the ever-ubiquitous Killdeer, and if we're lucky we may get one or two among Semipalmated Plover, Baird's Sandpiper, Semipalmated Sandpiper, Long-billed/Short-billed Dowitcher, Greater/Lesser Yellowlegs, or a number of other possible (though less likely) species! Scopes are helpful for these birds since they'll be viewed at enough of a distance that makes it tougher to identify with just binoculars; the group leader will have one for people to share, and anyone else who has a scope is encouraged to bring one. Additionally, depending on the group size and preference and the amount of activity/diversity of shorebirds that are present, we may also walk some of the nearby wooded trails. Whether or not we do, we'll still point out any interesting non-shorebirds that are in the area as well - swallows, terns, falcons, Osprey, and kingfishers are a handful that might be seen here.
location: həʔapus Village Park & Shoreline Habitat,4500 Duwamish River Trl, Seattle, WA 98106, USA

title: Online Class Series + Optional Field Trip: Shorebirds with David Droppers
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/shorebirdsdroppers2026

Shorebirds are not popular with beginning birders because they're so similar, so variable, and such a challenge. Shorebirds are popular with experts for the exact same reasons! So don't be intimidated by these fascinating, and frankly cute, group of birds that grace our shores (and other habitats) for brief periods of the year. We'll focus on identification, but more so, teach you tricks that willl make tackling shorebirds much less intimidating. A field trip to the coast during shorebird migration will help solidify what we learn in class. Move from beginner to expert so you can get excited about shorebird season!

FIELD TRIP: We'll travel to the coast, seeking different habitats to find different shorebirds. Our main event will be Bottle Beach as the tide comes in, but we'll also visit marinas, rocky dikes, and other locations from Bottle Beach to Tokeland. There will be an announcement a couple of days before the trip outlining details, particularly forecast, but plan to bring a lunch and be out for most of the day. We'll meet at the Green Lake Park n Ride at 6:30 AM and carpool from there. Drivers should have a Discover Pass for parking at Bottle Beach.

Online Classes: Thursdays, August 13, 20, and 27 from 7-8:30pm

Field Trip: Saturday, August 29 to Westport (Bottle Beach, Grayland Beach, Tokeland)

description:

Did you know Green Lake is a rich and diverse urban birding habitat? Over 175 species of birds have been seen at this eBird hot spot! During our outing we will explore migratory bird species as well as the many birds seen here all year long. All levels of birding experience and ages as well as families are welcome.

Green Lake monthly bird outings are inclusive of all ages, gender pronouns, sexual orientations, races, cultures, abilities, ethnicity and socioeconomic status. All participants are free to attend the outing for as long as they like. Our pace will be slow and easy as we navigate 2.8 miles of level terrain.

Dress appropriately for the weather and please leave dogs at home so as to not scare our feathered friends.

*Bring plenty of water and snacks, as this is a longer walk!*

A small parking lot is available and gets crowded, please leave time to park. Nearby street parking is also available. Green Lake is bus accessible with buses #62 and #45. A small bike rack is near the Café.

The trail is ADA accessible; all abilities and mobilities are welcome! Restrooms and drinking fountains are available at the nearby Green Lake Community Center Building and at various locations along the trail.

title: Online Class Series + Optional Field Trip: Shorebirds with David Droppers
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/shorebirdsdroppers2026

Shorebirds are not popular with beginning birders because they're so similar, so variable, and such a challenge. Shorebirds are popular with experts for the exact same reasons! So don't be intimidated by these fascinating, and frankly cute, group of birds that grace our shores (and other habitats) for brief periods of the year. We'll focus on identification, but more so, teach you tricks that willl make tackling shorebirds much less intimidating. A field trip to the coast during shorebird migration will help solidify what we learn in class. Move from beginner to expert so you can get excited about shorebird season!

FIELD TRIP: We'll travel to the coast, seeking different habitats to find different shorebirds. Our main event will be Bottle Beach as the tide comes in, but we'll also visit marinas, rocky dikes, and other locations from Bottle Beach to Tokeland. There will be an announcement a couple of days before the trip outlining details, particularly forecast, but plan to bring a lunch and be out for most of the day. We'll meet at the Green Lake Park n Ride at 6:30 AM and carpool from there. Drivers should have a Discover Pass for parking at Bottle Beach.

Online Classes: Thursdays, August 13, 20, and 27 from 7-8:30pm

Field Trip: Saturday, August 29 to Westport (Bottle Beach, Grayland Beach, Tokeland)

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Ballard Locks
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: John Leszczynski & Elaine Chuang

Meeting Point: In front of the Ballard Locks Visitor Center. For those driving, note that on the Ballard side of the Locks, there is a paid parking lot at the main entrance, and limited nearby street parking. There is free parking in a small lot in Commodore Park on the Magnolia side (south side).

Bike Parking: Bike racks near visitor center

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly solid pavement but some steps and steep hills) ~ 1 mil

The meeting of salt and fresh water at the Ballard Locks makes for an interesting mix of species, many of which can be observed at close range. Join us the last Saturday of each month to watch an ever-changing cast of birds. Seasonal visitors integrate with year-round residents like Belted Kingfishers, Cormorants, and Great Blue Herons. Cared for by the horticulture staff, the surrounding grounds are also habitat for “the usual suspects” for our region. This is a great walk for all, including beginners: no experience is necessary! If you can, bring binoculars; they are available on loan from the Birds Connect Seattle Retail Space located at 616 Olive Way, Seattle, WA. Please call 206-523-4483 to reserve a pair.
location: Ballard Locks Visitor Center,3015 NW 54th St, Seattle, WA 98107, USA

title: Children's Neighborhood Bird Outing: Meadowbrook Pond Natural Area
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.


Leader: Kathy Kaplan (she/her)

Meeting Point: 9:00am, Meadowbrook Community Center, 10517 35th Ave NE, Seattle, WA

Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), C (sidewalks and trail), less than 1 mile walking. Stroller friendly. If we do the full loop (which is TBD based on group that day), there is one spot that has 6 steps going down - with plenty of help available to get stroller down that way if needed. Bathroom at community center meeting point usually opens 9am Saturdays.


Calling all kiddos! This trip is geared for kids age 6 - 14, but all are welcome! Birding game will be included. All children must be accompanied by an adult. August is the tail end of the quiet Seattle birding summers, so we will practice looking and listening for our common Seattle birds and those returning or passing through for early fall migration.

Meadowbrook Pond is a wonderful little sanctuary which features diverse wildlife, interesting foliage, sculptures and trails all integrated so that humans and wildlife alike can enjoy the surroundings. It also plays a very practical role, capturing sediment from Thornton Creek and reducing downstream impact. The natural area features a wetland, wildlife habitat and open space. Bridges and walking platforms extend across the pond enabling visitors to get closer to the wildlife living in and visiting the pond. We hope to encounter a mix of passerines, raptors, and waterfowl. Our route will cover no more than 1 mile and usually takes ~1.5 hours, depending on what we see and hear. Dress for weather; bring binoculars, water & snacks.
location: Meadowbrook Community Center,10517 35th Ave NE, Seattle, WA 98125, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Next Gen at Marymoor Park (for Young Adults <40)
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary. Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions. Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Brett Eaton (he/him), Jordan Juranek (she/her), Pedro Rodriguez (he/him) and Emily Sellinger (she/her)--NextGen Council Members

Meeting Point: Marymoor Park Parking Lot G. 6046 West Lake Sammamish Parkway Northeast, Redmond, Washington 98052. $1 to park.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (soft ground, dirt). We will walk at a leisurely pace on mostly flat terrain and cover a distance of around ~2 miles. Birders of all levels, especially beginners are welcome. Please bring binoculars, we will have a couple extra pairs. Portable toilets are available in the park. There is a $1 fee to park at Marymoor Park and there are pay kiosks situated at each parking area throughout the park. Restrooms at the start of our route.


Join NextGen Council members Jordan, Pedro, Emily, and Brett for birding at Marymoor Park! We will have binoculars to borrow, spotting scopes to utilize, and guidebooks at the ready. We will enjoy the park from 9-11am, with an optional after-walk hangout at nearby PostDoc Brewing around 11:30am (brewery is all ages, alcoholic and non-alcoholic options for purchase, outside food ok, food truck on-site).

Birders of all levels, especially beginners are welcome. Please bring binoculars; we will have a few extra pairs. Portable toilets are available in the park at the start and end of our route. There is a $1 fee to park and payable at the kiosks situated at each parking area.

Considered one of the birding hotspots of the East side, Marymoor Park features views of Lake Sammamish, the Sammamish river trail, and a diverse population of birds. This NBO is specifically designed for adults in their 20s and 30s, and welcomes bird enjoyers of all experience levels. Please try to arrive by the listed time for introductions. If late, look for a group of people holding binoculars! Join us for drinks afterwards at PostDoc Brewing. Rain or shine, please bring rain gear. Please leave dogs at home. Bird photography encouraged.

Marymoor Park is located nearby to bus stops and light rail stations.
location: Marymoor Park Parking Lot G,6046 West Lake Sammamish Pkwy NE, Redmond, WA 98052, USA

title: BIRD UNIVERSITY Online Class - Bird by Ear: Laying the Foundation for a Lifetime of Listening with Whitney Neufeld-Kaiser
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/birdu_bbe2026

Online Classes: Tuesdays, September 1 and 8, from 6:30 - 8:30pm

Field Trip: Sunday, September 13, from 7-9:00 AM - location TBD

Bird by Ear: Laying the Foundation for a Lifetime of Listening

Identifying birds by their songs and calls can transform both your everyday life and your birding experience. It's also an integral part of Bird University. But how do you get started on this project, which may last your lifetime? This 2-part class will cover the foundations of active, analytical listening and introduce you to resources and techniques for learning how to identify birds by ear. The optional field trip will give you a chance to practice what you've learned.

Learn more about instructor Whitney https://birdsconnectsea.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Whitney-Neufeld-Kaiser.pdf

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Union Bay Natural Area
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Ed Dominguez (he/him)

Meeting Point: Center for Urban Horticulture in the East parking lot off NE 41st Street, one block beyond the place where Mary Gates Memorial Drive turns left to become NE 41st Street

Bike Parking: no

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails, with some soft ground or mud), ~1.5 miles walking

Union Bay Natural Area, just east of the University of Washington, offers a range of habitats: wetland to forest to open fields. Likely birds include Bald Eagle, Cooper's Hawk, belted kingfisher, and Savannah Sparrow. Spring migration will be in full swing with warblers, vireos, and tanagers singing. We will focus on developing birding by ear skills to help everyone with identifying these colorful visitors from the tropics. All are welcome from beginners to advanced birders; I will have something for everyone! Bring your birding questions and I will do my best share answers.
location: Union Bay Natural Area,3501 NE 41st St, Seattle, WA 98195, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Birds and Habitat – Along Arboretum Creek
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Larry Hubbell (he/him)

Meeting Point: The Birch Parking Lot – on the east side of Lake Washington Blvd. This is the only parking lot that is north of the Japanese Garden and south of the Boyer Ave / Lake Washington Blvd intersection.

Bike Parking: Yes. There is a bike rack.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails with some soft ground or mud)

We walk north approximately 1.5 miles through the Arboretum, often next to or parallel with Arboretum Creek and usually end up near Duck Bay (south of Foster Island). We will be searching for birds and discussing their habitat choices for food, nesting and roosting. We begin at 8:30 am and the trip will end around 10:30 am near Duck Bay – which is about one mile north of the Birch Parking Lot. However, walking south, back to your vehicle, may take another 20-30 minutes depending on each individual’s motivation. To learn more about our efforts to enhance the Arboretum Creek habitat for birds and nature please visit: www.arboretumcreek.org https://www.arboretumcreek.org

Photo Credit: Rod Fung
location: Birch Parking lot, Washington Park Arboretum

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Olympic Sculpture Park and Pier 70
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Tom Leschine (he/him) and Dave Galvin (he/him) 206-718-0054
Meeting Point: PACCAR PAVILION main entrance at Corner of Western and Broad. 2901 Western Ave, Seattle 98121

Trip Accessibility: Pace 1, Terrain B. Leisurely walking approx. 1/2 mile total. One downhill section to waterfront via steps or chip trail of moderate steepness. Many participants walk uphill back to PACCAR Pavilion area at end of outing, where they've parked their cars.

The OSP is mostly open and grassy with interspersed plantings of native vegetation and attracting fall and winter passerines typical of such urban environments. Occasional raptors present with the usual gulls and crows overhead. Pier 70 is completely different, featuring the seabird species that typically overwinter in Central Puget Sound. Expect a variety of overwintering gulls, diving ducks, alcids, and grebes, many close in and providing good views. Occasional surprises pop up in either location. For directions and more information go to the Olympic Sculpture Park http://www.seattleartmuseum.org/visit/olympic-sculpture-park website.
location: Paccar Pavilion,2901 Western Ave, Seattle, WA 98121, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Green Lake
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Roniq Bartanen (she/her)

Meeting Point: Green lake Boathouse in front of the café. 7351 East Green Lake Dr. N, Seattle, WA 98115. Green Lake café hours: Dependent on season

Bike Parking: A small bike rack is available near the coffee shop

Trip Accessibility: 1( leisurely pace), A ( paved and level), ~ 2.8 miles walking total loop

Did you know Green Lake is a rich and diverse urban birding habitat? Over 175 species of birds have been seen at this eBird hot spot! During our outing we will explore migratory bird species as well as the many birds seen here all year long. All levels of birding experience and ages as well as families are welcome.

Green Lake monthly bird outings are inclusive of all ages, gender pronouns, sexual orientations, races, cultures, abilities, ethnicity and socioeconomic status. All participants are free to attend the outing for as long as they like. Our pace will be slow and easy as we navigate 2.8 miles of level terrain.

Dress appropriately for the weather and please leave dogs at home so as to not scare our feathered friends.

*Bring plenty of water and snacks, as this is a longer walk!*

A small parking lot is available and gets crowded, please leave time to park. Nearby street parking is also available. Green Lake is bus accessible with buses #62 and #45. A small bike rack is near the Café.

The trail is ADA accessible; all abilities and mobilities are welcome! Restrooms and drinking fountains are available at the nearby Green Lake Community Center Building and at various locations along the trail.

Photo Credit: Roniq Bartanen

description:
This event is open to ALL AGES!

Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Emily Sellinger (she/her), Jordan Juranek (she/her), Pedro Rodriguez and Brett Eaton (he/him) NextGen Council members

Meeting Point: Carkeek Park: the parking lot closest to the beach, next to the playground. 950 NW Carkeek Park Rd, Seattle, WA 98177

Trip Accessibility: Trip Accessibility: 1, A, flat pavement, optional grass area. No walking. We will be set up near the park benches right next to the ADA parking spots. title: NextGen Wingspan Game Night @ Bizarre Brewing
description:
REGISTER https://secure.birdsconnectsea.org/a/nextgen-wingspan-game-night-september2026-bizarre

Date/Time: Wednesday, September 16, 6:30-9 p.m.

Location: Bizarre Brewing 4441 26TH AVE W SEATTLE, WA 98199

Join Birds Connect Seattle's NextGen Council for an evening of board game fun, at one of our favorite breweries - Bizarre Brewing!

This event is hosted by NextGen, but gamers of all ages are welcome to register and attend. We will set up the board games and split into groups from ~6:30-6:45pm, and start playing right around 6:45/7pm so we wrap up by 9pm (when the brewery closes).

Wingspan is a massively popular, competitive, medium-weight, card-driven, engine-building board game from Stonemaier Games. It's designed by Elizabeth Hargrave and features over 170 birds illustrated by Beth Sobel, Natalia Rojas, Ana Maria Martinez, and Martha Clare.

In the game (as in real life), you are bird enthusiasts—researchers, bird watchers, ornithologists, and collectors—seeking to discover and attract the best birds to your network of wildlife habitats. Each bird extends a chain of powerful combinations in one of your habitats (actions). Beginners are welcome!

When you arrive, please look for the table tents with our event name on them (Birds Connect Seattle Board Game Night) or ask one of the friendly beertenders for assistance. If you own the Wingspan board game, please bring it to the event, we will need some extra sets of the game and any expansion packs!

Bizarre Brewing: The taproom is all ages and well-behaved, leashed dog friendly. The taproom has two all-gender ADA restrooms. Outside food is welcome. Bizarre also offers snacks for purchase including: Umami Kushi giant hot pretzel twist w/mustard, Damoori Kitchen pita chips + hummus, Bavarian Meats landjaeger, Beecher’s Cheese cheese sticks, Cougar Mountain cookies, and Tim's chips. Alcoholic and non-alcoholic drink options available. Ample street parking nearby.

Please Note: This event has limited slots, and you must RSVP. If the event is full, please signup for the waitlist, and you will be moved up as people update their RSVPs.

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Montlake/Portage Bay
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Cynthia Putnam (she/her) & Kathy Kaplan (she/her)

Meeting Point: Montlake Community Center, 1618 East Calhoun St. Street parking in residential neighborhood on East Calhoun St. or in community center parking lot. Metro Transit Buses 43, 48 and 49.

Bike Parking: Bike racks are available in the front of the community center.

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), C (sidewalks and hiking trails), approx. 1 mile walking

No mask requirements

This walk encompasses Montlake Playfield Park and West Montlake Park, providing opportunities to explore birds from several vantage points. It is good for viewing waterfowl in Portage Bay as well as woodland birds in the neighborhood parks. A portion of the walk lends itself to finding birds by their sounds, so we hope to practice our birding by ear skills. Our route will cover no more than 2 miles and usually takes 2 hours, depending on what we see and hear. Dress for weather; bring binoculars, water & snacks. Over by 10:00am.
location: 1618 E Calhoun St, Seattle, WA 98112, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: BIPOC Birding at Lake Sammamish + optional Pot Luck
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Suzannah Yu (she/her) & Anthony Pangelinan (he/him)

Meeting Point: 9:00 am; Lake Sammamish State Park; southwest parking lot by the Rotunda Shelter; GPS: 47°33'18.6"N 122°04'04.8"W

Bike Parking: None expected.

Trip Accessibility: 1B

Masks: Optional

All-ages BIPOC Neighborhood Bird Outing with optional potluck afterwards

IMPORTANT: parking requires a Discover Pass or day pass. Pay stations for passes are present on-site. If you are interested in carpooling, feel free to request or offer a ride here: docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1wZe87hvmgoWHCXYret1tmEc1iPYuFFDm-UBn0x8othI/edit?usp=sharing https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1wZe87hvmgoWHCXYret1tmEc1iPYuFFDm-UBn0x8othI/edit?usp=sharing

BIPOC-centered neighborhood bird outing led by NextGen council members, Suzannah (she/her/hers) and Anthony (he/him/his). This is an all-ages walk. We will meet by the parking lot by the Rotunda Shelter. We hope to see a wide variety of birds, including wood ducks, mergansers, kingfishers, and woodpeckers. Birding wraps up around 12pm.

We will also be hosting a small optional potluck after the walk, from 12pm to about 1pm. Feel free to bring snacks and desserts, or anything that you'd like to share with the group. We will bring a treat, so come join for the social even if bringing something is not accessible for you. If you do choose to bring any food, please let the group know of any potential food sensitivities such as eggs, gluten, nuts, and dairy.

*Please note that this trip is specifically for folks who identify as Black, Indigenous, and People of Color (BIPOC)*

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Union Bay Natural Area
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: Julia Hansbrough & Dick Holcomb & Shelly Evans

Meeting Point: Center for Urban Horticulture in the East parking lot off NE 41st Street, one block beyond the place where Mary Gates Memorial Drive turns left to become NE 41st Street

Bike Parking: Yes; bike rack in back of building

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly gravel trails, with some soft ground or mud), ~1.5 miles walking

Union Bay Natural Area, just east of the University of Washington, offers a range of habitats: wetland to forest to open fields. Likely birds this time of year include wood duck, savannah sparrow, and osprey, possible birds include common yellowthroat, marsh wren, and Wilson's warbler.

title: Community Speaker Series - Birdbrains: A Lyrical Evening with Susan Rich, Stephaney Delaney, and others
description:
Registration coming soon

Date: Thursday, September 24, 6:30pm

Location: Birds Connect Seattle (616 Olive Way)

Birdbrains: A Lyrical Guide to Washington State Birds https://birdsconnectsea.myshopify.com/products/birdbrains-a-lyrical-guide-to-washington-state-birds?_pos=1&_sid=d791ce236&_ss=r is a one-of-a-kind Washington State bird guide. The anthology includes original pen and ink sumi-e paintings by artist Hiroko Seki, bird fact notes, and short poems/stories/memoir. Poems/prose are bird-focused rather than human centered. The bird notes include information about song, call, mating rituals, habitat, migration, climate, dark comedy, and fun facts.

Join us for an evening of conversation and poetry from several contributors to this anthology, including editor Susan Rich, bird bio author Stephanie Delaney, and several contributing poets.

Purchase your copy of the book here https://birdsconnectsea.myshopify.com/products/birdbrains-a-lyrical-guide-to-washington-state-birds?_pos=1&_sid=d791ce236&_ss=r .
location: Birds Connect Seattle headquarters and retail space (ground floor presentation area),616 Olive Wy, Seattle, WA 98101, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Ballard Locks
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leaders: John Leszczynski & Elaine Chuang

Meeting Point: In front of the Ballard Locks Visitor Center. For those driving, note that on the Ballard side of the Locks, there is a paid parking lot at the main entrance, and limited nearby street parking. There is free parking in a small lot in Commodore Park on the Magnolia side (south side).

Bike Parking: Bike racks near visitor center

Trip Accessibility: 1 (leisurely pace), B (mostly solid pavement but some steps and steep hills) ~ 1 mil

The meeting of salt and fresh water at the Ballard Locks makes for an interesting mix of species, many of which can be observed at close range. Join us the last Saturday of each month to watch an ever-changing cast of birds. Seasonal visitors integrate with year-round residents like Belted Kingfishers, Cormorants, and Great Blue Herons. Cared for by the horticulture staff, the surrounding grounds are also habitat for “the usual suspects” for our region. This is a great walk for all, including beginners: no experience is necessary! If you can, bring binoculars; they are available on loan from the Birds Connect Seattle Retail Space located at 616 Olive Way, Seattle, WA. Please call 206-523-4483 to reserve a pair.
location: Ballard Locks Visitor Center,3015 NW 54th St, Seattle, WA 98107, USA

title: Neighborhood Bird Outing: Hidden trails of West Seattle/Delridge (Camp Long, Longfellow Greenspace)
description:
Neighborhood Bird Outings are open to all with no pre-registration required. Group size will vary.

Outings occur rain or shine, though will be canceled for dangerous weather conditions.

Please leave pets at home.

Leader: Kurt Aylup (he/him) 248-227-1407

Meet: 10 am, High Point Pond (called "Pond Park" on Google Maps), 5911 High Point Dr SW

Trip Accessibility: 1-2 (leisurely to moderate pace), C (hiking trails with gentle, rolling hills), distance ~2-3 miles

Onsite Bike Parking: No known bike parking locations, however there are railings/benches that may work as a spot to park a bike if needed.

The West Seattle/Delridge area has several large, wooded areas with numerous trails winding through them, many of which are not particularly frequent birder destinations. One of those areas is through Longfellow Creek and nearby High Point Pond, which between the two of them includes a nice mix of habitats - a pond in an open area, dense understory, wooded trails, and clearings adjacent to said trails. This is a great place to check out what species may be migrating south during the fall. The two walks that we've done here so far both had a nice variety of species show up - we got almost 40 species here on the walk from the end of May! We'll start at Pond Park for a central meeting spot that should make potential parking spots closer (the H, 5, and 21 bus lines also stop not far from it), and then walk through some of the Longfellow Creek trails. If there's time, we might also hit Greg Davis park or some Camp Long trails.
location: 5911 High Point Dr SW, Seattle, WA 98126, USA
